abstract: Energy agendas in national parliaments are crucial when countries seek to develop their energy futures while adhering to international obligations. This article examines how energy agendas emerge and evolve in parliamentary debates using data from Finland over a period of 12 years. By relying on topic modelling, we can show how the key energy agendas relate to an overall energy solution, promoting domestic energy production and seeking carbonneutral energy, and how they evolve successively alongside general concerns for the country's task ahead in the field. Examining more detailed agendas, in turn, validates the first analysis and exposes some differences in the agendas of political parties. These differences were few. This further specifies the nature of Finnish energy politics, which is often considered consensual except for nuclear power and peat as sources of energy. The article demonstrates that scholars who examine parliamentary politics may benefit from the use of language-based computational methodologies to uncover insights that have previously been difficult to attain.

sections.paper_id: Mykkänen et al. - 2024 - Energy agendas A longitudinal analysis of Finnish parliamentary debates
paragraphs: Parliaments in liberal democracies play a key role in determining the conditions under which the energy sector evolves. Political agendas in parliamentary energy debates relate to a wide spectrum of issues, such as energy prices and secure access to energy, as well as competition between different energy sources Scand Political Stud. 2024;47:97-121.

wileyonlinelibrary.com/journal/scps | (Debus & Tosun, 2021;Karhunmaa, 2019;Schmid, 2021;Schmidt et al., 2019). Related changes in environmental concerns and technological developments have prompted redefinitions of political energy agendas over time. For instance, the mitigation of climate change has attracted strong political interest in solar and wind energy while prompting new interest in nuclear power (Edberg & Tarasova, 2016;Matschoss & Repo, 2020;Nuttall, 2022). The Russian attack on Ukraine in 2022 has further changed energy politics in Europe and contributed to sustainable energy transition as a strengthening political agenda (Steffen & Patt, 2022).

When examining national energy politics, the focus on decision-making and policy formulation are understandably of interest, but they fall short of accounting for the underlying debates (see, e.g., Karhunmaa, 2019). Focusing on government decision processes also makes it difficult to construe how parties' long-term energy positions change over time. Likewise, examining voting data (Buzogány & Ćetković, 2021) is often inadequate because parliamentarians' voting behaviour is typically controlled by their parties (Bowler et al., 1999;Döring, 1995). Looking at voting behaviour or using coded textual data (Schmid, 2021) have been popular approaches, partly due to methodological limitations. The situation is now changing with advances in machine learning and natural language processing, which have enabled scholars to examine parliamentarians' speeches without resorting to samples or coded data (Ivanusch, 2023;Müller-Hansen et al., 2021;Repo et al., 2021;Ristilä & Elo, 2023). These methodologies are particularly useful when construing how parties' long-term energy positions change over time.

Using computational methodologies, we analyse full transcripts from Finnish parliamentary debates on energy that cover a relatively long period (2008)(2009)(2010)(2011)(2012)(2013)(2014)(2015)(2016)(2017)(2018)(2019)(2020) and thus account for potential shifts in political agendas. This approach allows us to identify both how political agendas proceed in connection with government changes and legislative developments and how the debates reflect varying party positions on energy matters. Based on its multiparty composition, the 200-member Finnish parliament is well suited for studying differences between parties and potential consolidation of politicians' attention to different policies. During our research period, representatives from nine parties held seats in the parliament (see Table A1), which increases the likelihood of pluralistic debates on energy issues.

Aside from political pluralism, the Finnish political debate on energy is interesting because it covers a wide range of energy alternatives, ranging from renewables to nuclear power, peat and other fossil fuels. In the European context, Finland has a high energy consumption per capita, and energy debates are a recurring topic in the Finnish parliament. Our research period was characterised by several energy issues, particularly important ones being nuclear energy (see, e.g., Lehtonen, 2020), domestic energy sources including biomass (see, e.g., Kivimaa & Mickwitz, 2011;Majava et al., 2022), climate change mitigation and energy security (e.g., Teräväinen et al., 2011), as well as providing cheap energy for heavy industry (e.g., Jensen-Eriksen, 2022;Lehtonen, 2020). The period can be described as an acceleration phase towards sustainable energy sources.

We argue that existing research on parliamentary agenda formation, even when carefully coding debate transcripts from long periods of time (e.g., Little, 2023) or when systematically analysing members' formal interventions (Gava et al., 2017), is somewhat limited in grasping the full spectrum of parliamentary speeches. In other words, by limiting research to institutionally or otherwise predefined debate categories, these studies leave out a potentially large amount of parliamentary talk. Members of parliament may use debates for issue competition (Ivanusch, 2023) or signalling purposes (Proksch & Slapin, 2012) besides aligning their messages with government or opposition or falling in line with party discipline (Finocchiaro & Rohde, 2008;Giannetti & Pedrazzani, 2016). This applies particularly to specific policy issues, such as energy politics, the agendas of which are neither static nor formed exclusively in debates dedicated to these policy issues. Our methodology captures changes in parliamentary agendas as well as energy-related speech turns that belong to agendas that are either part of formal debates on energy or fall outside the explicit energy debates. We will ask what the key agendas have been in the Finnish parliamentary debates on energy and how they have changed over time. We also consider these changes in relation to shifts in government coalitions and compare party positions on energy agendas.

We will first describe parliaments as contexts for political agenda formation and further discuss our research questions and expected findings, followed by an overview of Finnish energy politics before moving to the empirical analysis of changing energy agendas. The latter is done in two parts: first, examining what energy agendas dominate the debates at which times, and second, how parties differ in their emphases. We will conclude by discussing the significance of our approach and findings for the study of parliamentary agendas in general and Finnish energy politics in particular.

sections.paper_id: Mykkänen et al. - 2024 - Energy agendas A longitudinal analysis of Finnish parliamentary debates
paragraphs: Although it is quite evident that the support of political parties is needed if policies towards changes in energy policies are to proceed, policymakers' attention must be drawn to relevant issues before the required political support can be given to these policies (Jones & Baumgartner, 2005;Repo et al., 2018). In the case of energy politics, their attention can potentially be directed to many related issues, such as climate change, energy security or taxation. One way to look at how attention to energy issues has been refocused is through parliamentary debates. Energy debates can become fragmented, highlight varying issues, and further constitute reactions prompted by political developments. They can also stabilise around long-lasting themes that appear relevant to most parliamentarians while being punctuated by briefly urgent matters (Jones & Baumgartner, 2012) or new knowledge or technologies (Cruz-Castro & Sanz-Menéndez, 2005). To observe the corresponding developments, we must first understand how parliamentary agendas are formed and controlled, and how that informs our choice of methodology.

More generally, in parliamentary democracies, parliaments routinely organise their work according to bills introduced by the government, to be discussed and decided in the parliament. Where governing coalitions form a detailed government policy programme, as is the case in Finland, parliamentary agendas are quite firmly set when the government takes office (De Winter, 1995). This formal method of agenda-setting is reinforced in the parliamentary committees, which, in majority governments, are dominated by members of the government parties. Therefore, parliamentary agendas are predominantly government-driven and vary with the governments' willingness and ability to execute their programmes. It should be noted that coalition governments are prone to prioritising bills that are unanimously supported by the coalition partners and delaying the less consensual ones (Martin, 2004), but this does not change the fact that the agenda-setting power remains with the government. Further important factors that directly affect parliamentary agendas are international agreements and transnational legislation, such as European law, which need to be ratified in parliament. However, there are many national variations in parliaments' ability to control political agendas (Döring & Doring, 2001). Parliaments can prioritise some bills over others in their sessional calendars and have in their rules of procedure a chance to introduce themes for debates irrespective of the respective governments' plans and legislative processes (e.g., private members' bills or question hours). Nevertheless, this does not alter the fact that governments dominate formal parliamentary agendas.

However, to fully understand parliamentary agenda-setting, we need a broader approach. Agenda formation also takes place in parliamentary debates as members of parliament compete with their ideas between and within political parties in the quest for political power and support of their constituencies. This type of agenda, which arises from political issue competition (Ivanusch, 2023), additionally consists of elements that appear independently of the formal agenda (i.e., the official theme of the debate). The parliamentary agenda in this sense is not fully predetermined but emerges when parliamentarians debate. Members of parliament have different interests and different vote-seeking strategies (Debus & Tosun, 2021). Further, political outcomes need not reflect party fragmentation (Buzogány & Ćetković, 2021). The Finnish parliament is a particularly interesting case due to Finland's tradition of relying on multiple energy sources (Kainiemi et al., 2020;Ruostetsaari, 2017), which gives parliamentarians numerous opportunities to attempt to shape the energy agenda and stand out in the policy field.

Recognising these complexities in parliamentary agenda-setting and making note of recent works (Ivanusch, 2023;Ristilä & Elo, 2023;Wäckerle & Castanho Silva, 2023), formulating theory-driven hypotheses or examining a wide range of parliamentary debates may readily contribute to findings that are of quite general character, with overarching topics such as 'agriculture', 'education' and 'environment' being observed. As our aim is to examine parliamentary agendas within one such field, namely 'energy', and observe the contextual complexities within that field, we proceed with a data-driven approach. Importantly, analysis of parliamentary agendas must recognise that agendas develop in parallel with reactions to government bills, and even beyond them.

The quite fragmented party structure in the Finnish parliament might tempt us to assume that policy debates exhibit considerable variance. However, as the Finnish government belongs to the majority coalition type, the government parties predominantly vote in unison on government bills. This effectively reduces pluralism in parties' and representatives' active policy positions. But this predominantly applies to formal parliamentary agendas and voting behaviour. When debates are approached in a broader sense, we witness greater political variety in the energy agendas. We progress from the general to the specific and first examine energy agendas and their lifespans based on electoral cycles and changes of government. Are there distinct agendas for each government term or can we observe simultaneously overlapping agendas that may reflect differing interests among members of parliament? Then, we break down more focused agendas according to political parties to account for distinguishable party positions, which may stem from parliamentarians' individual reasoning rather than formal procedures in parliament or voting behaviour, which is often restricted by party discipline and loyalty to the government coalition. To what extent we can observe distinctive party agendas and how well do these (if any) agendas reflect the parties' traditional positions? Finally, based on our analyses, we will discuss the nature of the consensus in Finnish energy politics.

To answer these questions by analysing a long period of parliamentary debates without omitting anything that may have contributed towards an energy agenda, we rely on the methodology of topic modelling. Topic modelling allows the screening of complete transcripts of parliamentary debates and is used to capture all speech irrespective of formal agendas. We focus on the topical structure of the debates and the differences between electoral terms and individual parties in the 12-year period between 2008 and 2020. In other words, we seek to find agendas that become dominant in parliamentary debates (Bosman et al., 2014;Wright et al., 2021). By dominant agendas, we mean agendas that overshadow others in the debate. We should be able to show where the dominant agenda was still in its infancy and how it took over earlier agendas in the debates.

When debates are approached in a broader sense, we witness greater political variety in the energy agendas. We progress from the general to the specific and first examine energy agendas and their lifespans based on electoral cycles and changes of government. Are there distinct agendas for each government term or can we observe simultaneously overlapping agendas that may reflect differing interests among members of parliament? Then, we break down more focused agendas according to political parties to account for distinguishable party positions, which may stem from parliamentarians' individual reasoning rather than formal procedures in parliament or voting behaviour, which is often restricted by party discipline and loyalty to the government coalition. To what extent we can observe distinctive party agendas and how well do these (if any) agendas reflect the parties' traditional positions? Finally, based on our analyses, we will discuss the nature of the consensus in Finnish energy politics.

To answer these questions by analysing a long period of parliamentary debates without omitting anything that may have contributed towards an energy agenda, we rely on the methodology of topic modelling. Topic modelling allows the screening of complete transcripts of parliamentary debates and is used to capture all speech irrespective of formal agendas. We focus on the topical structure of the debates and the differences between electoral terms and individual parties in the 12-year period between 2008 and 2020. In other words, we seek to find agendas that become dominant in parliamentary debates (Bosman et al., 2014;Wright et al., 2021). By dominant agendas, we mean agendas that overshadow others in the debate. We should be able to show where the dominant agenda was still in its infancy and how it took over earlier agendas in the debates.
sections.preview_head: Although it is quite evident that the support of political parties is needed if policies towards changes in energy policies are to proceed, policymakers' attention must be drawn to relevant issues before the required political support can be given to these policies (Jones & Baumgartner, 2005;Repo et al., 2018). In the case of energy politics, their attention can potentially be directed to many related issues, such as climate change, energy security or taxation. One way to look at how attention to energy issues has been refocused is through parliamentary debates.

sections.paper_id: Mykkänen et al. - 2024 - Energy agendas A longitudinal analysis of Finnish parliamentary debates
paragraphs: Finland has relied on a broad selection of energy sources, allowing many political preferences to flourish. Besides fossil fuels, hydropower and nuclear power-and more recently solar and wind power and geothermal energy-have been part of the energy mix. Heavy industry has traditionally been the single most important consumer of energy and, as a consequence, the Finnish energy politics debate has been influenced by strong industry interest in low-priced energy (e.g., Jensen-Eriksen, 2022;Lehtonen, 2020).

Finland has mostly been rather consensus-seeking in that negotiations and debates have been considered important before deciding on energy policies (Kainiemi et al., 2020;Ruostetsaari, 2017;Vikström et al., 2023). The years under consideration in this article relate to a period when the transition towards novel renewable energies started to gain ground and accelerated. Fossil fuels have lost share in energy consumption and investment in renewable energy sources has increased. 1 Energy use per capita in Finland is high: 5.8 toe/capita in 2020, which is twice the European Union (EU) average (Enerdata, 2020), and there is a recognised need to reduce emissions from energy use. The national goal was to increase the share of renewable energy use of final energy consumption to more than 50% during the 2020s (Ministry of Economic Affairs and Employment, 2022). Roughly 40% of energy was produced by renewable energy sources (including biofuels and waste, wind, solar and hydro, 140 TWh) and 20% by nuclear energy (68 TWh) in 2021 (LUKE, 2022). In 2019, the use of coal in energy production after 2029 was banned (Finlex, 2019). Finnish energy policy is further guided by strategies that are the responsibility of different ministries. A long-term energy and climate strategy adopted at least every 10 years is the responsibility of the Ministry of Economic Affairs and Employment, and a midterm climate policy strategy is set for each legislative period of the Ministry of the Environment. Key national energy policy processes discussed in the Finnish parliament are presented in Table 1.

Energy policy in Finland is also subject to European regulations. Directives set by the EU are adopted in national legislation and are thereby discussed and processed in parliament. Important European policy processes affecting the energy sector during the period have included two Climate and Energy Packages. The first was set in April 2009 to promote the use of energy from renewable sources (EC, 2009). The second was set in October 2014, with the binding EU target to reduce domestic greenhouse gas emissions by at least 40% by 2030 compared to 1990 (EUCO, 2014). The targets were re-examined when the EU joined the Paris Agreement, with new binding emission reduction targets. Upon the ratification of the Paris Agreement, the agreement and its goals were discussed in parliament at the end of 2016. To achieve these goals, the responsibilities of key actors and an emission reduction target were set in a national climate act in 2015. Other significant energy policy processes comply with the European directives on Energy Performance of Buildings (EU, 2018a) and Energy Efficiency and Promotion of Renewable Energy (EU, 2018b), and the EC (2020).

International events also affect energy politics, although seldom in straightforward ways. For instance, the global financial crisis in 2007-2008 led to hikes in Finnish energy taxation as the government needed additional revenues due to reallocations in labour market expenditures. Surprisingly, the Fukushima nuclear accident in Japan did not greatly impact the Finnish determination to construct more nuclear energy plants (Ylönen et al., 2017). Even though Russia seized Crimea from Ukraine in 2014 and instigated separatist movements in the Donetsk and Luhansk areas, which raised international political concerns over energy security, this had hardly any impact on Finland's energy policy and imports from Russia. Some Finnish energy producers also started cooperating with the Russian nuclear energy company Rosatom to build the Hanhikivi nuclear power plant in Northern Finland. Some members of the Finnish parliament and Finnish members of the European Parliament expressed their opposition to the project, but construction continued as there was political will to increase nuclear power capacity (see, e.g., Lehtonen, 2020;Tynkkynen, 2016). Amidst the Russian attack on Ukraine in 2022 (e.g., Steffen & Patt, 2022), construction was eventually terminated.

sections.paper_id: Mykkänen et al. - 2024 - Energy agendas A longitudinal analysis of Finnish parliamentary debates
paragraphs: Our research examines parliamentary energy debates using machine learning and natural language processing. Methodologically, we rely on the topic modelling of Finnish parliamentary debates on the theme of energy between 2008 and 2020. Our task is to extract and analyse agendas in the form of topics allocated in the debates. To be clear, agendas are understood here as one or more topics that dominate the parliamentary debate at any point in time. Two instances of modelling with Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) are performed. Topic modelling is complemented by statistical analysis of variance. To test the differences between parties and annual legislative sessions, F tests were used when the group means were based on equal variance. In cases where this assumption was violated, the Brown-Forsythe test was used instead (Brown & Forsythe, 1974).

As data, we use complete transcripts from all plenary debates of the Finnish parliament from 9 September 2008 to 25 June 2020. These data were collected first from the Finnish language bank Korp(2017, which contained transcripts until 2016, and from then onwards manually from the records of the Finnish parliament. We used the search string 'energ*' in a concordance search, which works well in the Finnish language. The search results construct a corpus consisting of sentences that relate to energy matters and cover specific concerns, such as the price of electricity or the use of fossil fuels when these are connected to energy debates through the search string. The data collection resulted in 23,264 sentences of textual data. Sentences are warranted units of textual analysis in this examination of parliamentarians' often wide-ranging speeches because they are more focused on the topic of interest than paragraphs or longer bodies of text. There are variations in how energy was debated over the years, with an average of 2140 sentences per full year, two highs of 5219 in 2010 and 3705 in 2014, and a slightly declining frequency. Our modelling examines agenda changes as topical shares, and these numbers are adequate for such analysis.

Texts in the corpus were preprocessed following standard procedures in the field, which included the removal of stop words and punctuation as well as formal procedures in parliamentary speeches. To take the highly morphological character of the Finnish language into account, the words of the analysed texts were lemmatised to their basic forms (Mäkelä, 2018). Swedish is an official language in Finland, and Swedish transcripts from the parliamentary sessions were translated into Finnish by the authors and then processed according to the same procedure.

Topic modelling with LDA was applied to identify topics characteristic of the parliamentary debates. Topic modelling is a probabilistic methodology for natural languages (Blei et al., 2003) and produces sets of co-occurring words, which constitute topics. It is suitable for the analysis of parliamentary debates that often cover a wide range of issues that are hence difficult to identify and categorise. The discovery of crosscutting issues across a large set of unstructured texts is a major strength of the methodology. Findings can further be positioned in timelines, as we will do. Our modellings of the topics were conducted with the machine learning language toolkit MALLET, in which document instances such as sentences per party and year are handled as files (Benites-Lazaro et al., 2018;McCallum, 2016). A particular limitation of topic modelling is that it does not differentiate between positive and negative stances on topics. Hence, we examine shares and distributions of topics as expressions of political interest.

An important research task in the use of the methodology is determining the number of topics to be used in modelling. This defines the granularity of the findings, with smaller numbers contributing to more general interpretations and larger numbers to more detailed findings. Algorithmic procedures have been suggested to determine the number of topics in documents, but whether an ideal number of topics exists in real-world applications has been questioned, and hence iterations are typically performed in the modelling process (Sbalchiero & Eder, 2020;Vayansky & Kumar, 2020;Zhao et al., 2015). In this sense, research designs and aims provide the heuristics for the range of topics. Similarly, semisupervised modelling approaches and coding procedures have also been suggested to overcome challenges relating to observing and labelling topics (Béchara et al., 2021;Ivanusch, 2023), but these are not a good fit for our research design, in which we rely on human judgement in the analysis of the full corpus of parliamentary speeches. Semisupervised modelling puts weight on the preanalysis stage, which may be problematic in terms of capturing variance in the data.

We make use of this opportunity to determine the modelled number of topics to address our two distinct research aims. First, we are interested in observing, through topic modelling, whether energy agendas, in general, have parallel or overlapping lifespans. This is an important research task, as it would imply that parliamentary energy agendas in general shift thematically or contextually rather than following parties' ideological positions. Our data covers 12 legislative sessions in parliamentary debates and four electoral terms (with elections taking place in 2011, 2015 and 2019), and it is quite conceivable that shifts in energy agendas follow electoral terms, thus being related to government policies. To explore such shifts, we model the data with four topics, assuming that each electoral term might be dominated by one dominant topic. Trials with smaller and larger numbers indicated that modelling the data with four topics revealed topics that were also substantially meaningful and distinct.

Second, we observe in greater detail how political parties emphasise energy topics. The many sources of energy and the security concerns relating to them, as well as their connections to climate change mitigation, offer many combinations on which the political parties can construct their energy perspectives. To cover a wider variety of party profiles and to identify potential shifts in the parties' profiles independent of electoral terms and government coalitions, we perform modelling with a larger number of topics than when examining the parliament's general and integrative focus. An intuitive way to proceed is to start by selecting one topic per parliamentary party, that is, nine topics. After trials, we determined that modelling with 10 topics covered the criteria of meaningfulness and distinctness better than nine topics.

sections.paper_id: Mykkänen et al. - 2024 - Energy agendas A longitudinal analysis of Finnish parliamentary debates
paragraphs: The two distinct modellings of parliamentary debates provide complementary findings, which are discussed in the upcoming sections. The findings from the first modelling instance allow us to explore how parliamentary energy topics evolve over time and further assess this against changes in government. sections.paper_id: Mykkänen et al. - 2024 - Energy agendas A longitudinal analysis of Finnish parliamentary debates
sections.header_raw: Detailed modelling shows few differences between parties
sections.header_norm: detailed modelling shows few differences between parties
paragraphs: A 10-topic modelling allows us to better reveal differences in how parties focus on energy topics in the parliamentary debates. It further serves to validate the identification of the key topics and the recognition of important ruptures in the debate timeline. A multistage labelling procedure considering modelling outcomes and diagnostics as well as involving the authors was followed, as was done in labelling the smaller number of topics. The 10 modelled topics are presented in Table 3 below.

Modelling with 10 topics indicates that the findings obtained are robust. The four topics identified earlier are still recognisable although their contents have become more specific, and the six additional topics have become detached from them (Figure 2). For instance, the share of the generic topic 'Finland's task ahead' has fallen from over 70% to approximately 50%. 'Carbon neutral energy', on the other hand, is now more singularly about climate change (hence the new name 'Climate change'), and two substantially related topics ('Green transition' and 'Role of energy in climate policy') emerge in the more nuanced modelling. Similarly, 'Energy package' and 'Energy taxation' cover the earlier topic of an overall energy solution. The energy package is the only topic involving nuclear energy but reflects an overarching solution in conjunction with renewables and energy efficiency, which were debated when the topic peaked in 2010 and to a lesser degree in 2014 on the principal decisions on nuclear power.

Taxation naturally belongs to a debate that aims at finding a balanced solution to the supply and demand of energy, and incidentally is the only topic with a direct reference to price, indicating that the price of energy in other settings seems to be an abstract underlying concept. 'Wood as a source of energy' is a special topic that has surfaced a few times. It relates to the topic that focuses on 'Domestic energy production', but its minor peaks do not overlap with that topic.

'Energy certificate for buildings' peaks in 2012 and covers around one-fifth of the plenary debates at that point. The topic is linked to the debate on the government bill on energy certificates that was passed in the parliament in December 2012. The bill was based on the EU directive on the subject and, as such, was a source of great political turmoil because it was thought to affect real estate market values negatively and introduce unwanted red tape. 'Energy efficiency' is a relatively minor topic in our timeline, being a quite visible topic in 2008 but plummeting soon after. It could be linked with the topic 'Energy package' as energy efficiency applies to the energy mix and is a factor that partly affects energy sources.

When we examine the results of the 10-topic modelling on a timeline (Figure 2), the highlights identified in the four-topic modelling can still be recognised. However, the newly identified topics evolve more sporadically and less sequentially. The years that stand out for 'Overall energy solution ' are 2008, 2009, and 2010 (p ≤ 0.05). 'Domestic energy production' differs statistically from other years in 2014 (p ≤ 0.05), and 'Climate change' from 2016 onwards (p ≤ 0.05). These years correspond roughly to the years identified in the model with four topics. Accounting for the results from both the 4-and 10-topic modellings, we can say with confidence that there seem to be life spans for energy topics in parliamentary debates when these are observed at a general level, but that a more detailed analysis reveals that there are also more minute topical and temporal differences in how debates evolve. We will make use of this observation when we turn our attention to the potential differences in how political parties contribute to the debates.

T A B L E 3 (Continued) Dirichlet parameter Topic 20 most relevant keywords method, law, consumption, live, knowledge, proposal, small house, certificate, improve, real, sense, citizens' initiative, can 0.257 Climate change Climate strategy, renewable energy, on its part, coal, million, energy tax, fossil, endure, return, traffic, give up, proposal, climate, climate change, renewable, clean, year, peat, in one's opinion 0.233 Wood as a source of energy Renewable energy, energy wood, subsidy, million, wood, forest, small wood, renew, proposal, usage, method, promote, part, energy subvention, bioenergy, food, agriculture, substance, peat, profitable Note: Finnish compound keywords appear as two or three words in English. In our four-topic model, the only statistically significant difference was found between the liberal Swedish People's Party and the conservative populist Finns Party on the topic 'Domestic energy production' (p ≤ 0.10). This is not surprising, as the Finns Party tends to promote national self-reliance and the importance of national sovereignty over globalisation and international energy markets (Repo et al., 2021). Otherwise, the modelling showed that parties appear to engage in energy debates in a similar fashion. This is not to say that the parties would always act in unison when debating energy issues in the parliament. In hoping to reveal these potential differences, we also applied a modelling with ten topics.

The 10-topic modelling, in contrast, gives a more nuanced view of the differences in party contributions to the energy debate. In Table 4, the parties are listed according to their deviations from the topic means of the 10 modelled topics. The clearest differences are encountered in the topics 'Green transition', 'Domestic energy production' and 'Wood as a source of energy', all of which are quite predictable given the traditional political preferences of the parties. 3 Findings show that the Greens have placed the 'Green transition' high on their agenda when distinguishing themselves from the other parties (p ≤ 0.001). The Left Alliance also shows similar yet more moderate interest in the 'Green transition'. The Swedish People's Party differs from the Centre in a similar fashion (p ≤ 0.01). The latter is by far the least active party on this topic. Traditionally, the green transition has been treated with suspicion by the Centre

and seen as a problematic trajectory for the party's core supporters in the farming sector. The Finns Party, being a conservative populist party, appears the most active party in stressing 'Domestic energy production', which is in line with the party's ideology of national self-reliance. This observation applies throughout the parties, but statistically significant differences appear only in relation to the Swedish People's Party and the Social Democratic Party (p ≤ 0.05).

'Wood as a source of energy' fits well with the Centre Party's policy line as the party of the countryside, where forestry forms a significant means of livelihood. Interestingly, the test statistics tell us that the difference in favour of the Centre Party is most pronounced against the political left, the Greens and the Swedish People's Party (p ≤ 0.05). We also observed a somewhat unexpected difference in the topic 'Role of energy in climate policy', in which the Swedish People's Party set itself apart from the rest (p ≤ 0.001). This could be explained by the party's keen interest in developing a clean tech cluster around the city of Vaasa, where Swedish speakers form a large minority.

Although the observed party differences were few and quite expected, the more detailed modelling with 10 topics was useful in corroborating the findings of the first modelling. To put it simply, two modellings give us both an underlying topic structure and more granularity. The methodological advantage is evident: a great deal of detail may hide something that a coarser analysis reveals, and vice versa. Of course, finding a good balance is case-specific and requires testing and contextual knowledge. In our case, we could have presented an even more finely tuned modelling to inspect special topicssuch as peat or tax issuesmore closely, but that falls outside the scope of this article. Similarly, further insights on party positions could have been achieved with additional use of natural language methodologies that take positive and negative stances into account.

In our case, we could have presented an even more finely tuned modelling to inspect special topicssuch as peat or tax issuesmore closely, but that falls outside the scope of this article. Similarly, further insights on party positions could have been achieved with additional use of natural language methodologies that take positive and negative stances into account.

A 10-topic modelling allows us to better reveal differences in how parties focus on energy topics in the parliamentary debates. It further serves to validate the identification of the key topics and the recognition of important ruptures in the debate timeline. A multistage labelling procedure considering modelling outcomes and diagnostics as well as involving the authors was followed, as was done in labelling the smaller number of topics. The 10 modelled topics are presented in Table 3 below.

Modelling with 10 topics indicates that the findings obtained are robust. The four topics identified earlier are still recognisable although their contents have become more specific, and the six additional topics have become detached from them (Figure 2). For instance, the share of the generic topic 'Finland's task ahead' has fallen from over 70% to approximately 50%. 'Carbon neutral energy', on the other hand, is now more singularly about climate change (hence the new name 'Climate change'), and two substantially related topics ('Green transition' and 'Role of energy in climate policy') emerge in the more nuanced modelling. Similarly, 'Energy package' and 'Energy taxation' cover the earlier topic of an overall energy solution. The energy package is the only topic involving nuclear energy but reflects an overarching solution in conjunction with renewables and energy efficiency, which were debated when the topic peaked in 2010 and to a lesser degree in 2014 on the principal decisions on nuclear power.

Taxation naturally belongs to a debate that aims at finding a balanced solution to the supply and demand of energy, and incidentally is the only topic with a direct reference to price, indicating that the price of energy in other settings seems to be an abstract underlying concept. 'Wood as a source of energy' is a special topic that has surfaced a few times. It relates to the topic that focuses on 'Domestic energy production', but its minor peaks do not overlap with that topic.

'Energy certificate for buildings' peaks in 2012 and covers around one-fifth of the plenary debates at that point. The topic is linked to the debate on the government bill on energy certificates that was passed in the parliament in December 2012. The bill was based on the EU directive on the subject and, as such, was a source of great political turmoil because it was thought to affect real estate market values negatively and introduce unwanted red tape. 'Energy efficiency' is a relatively minor topic in our timeline, being a quite visible topic in 2008 but plummeting soon after. It could be linked with the topic 'Energy package' as energy efficiency applies to the energy mix and is a factor that partly affects energy sources.

When we examine the results of the 10-topic modelling on a timeline (Figure 2), the highlights identified in the four-topic modelling can still be recognised. However, the newly identified topics evolve more sporadically and less sequentially. The years that stand out for 'Overall energy solution ' are 2008, 2009, and 2010 (p ≤ 0.05). 'Domestic energy production' differs statistically from other years in 2014 (p ≤ 0.05), and 'Climate change' from 2016 onwards (p ≤ 0.05). These years correspond roughly to the years identified in the model with four topics. Accounting for the results from both the 4-and 10-topic modellings, we can say with confidence that there seem to be life spans for energy topics in parliamentary debates when these are observed at a general level, but that a more detailed analysis reveals that there are also more minute topical and temporal differences in how debates evolve. We will make use of this observation when we turn our attention to the potential differences in how political parties contribute to the debates.

sections.paper_id: Mykkänen et al. - 2024 - Energy agendas A longitudinal analysis of Finnish parliamentary debates
paragraphs: The agendas that appear in parliamentary debates tell us a great deal about the political interests that underlie decision-making and policy outcomes. Topic modelling, the computational method used here, enabled us to identify such agendas and to examine how they evolved over twelve parliamentary sessions and across political parties in Finland. Most importantly, we were able to identify agendas in the parliamentary energy debates that took dominant positions by replacing previous topics, and when this happened. Our results show that the focus of energy policy was quite stable during the parliamentary terms. There were no major interruptions that changed the main lines of the debates during each parliamentary term.

Methodologically, we modelled the textual data rather than categorising the debates beforehand. This method allowed us to approach parliamentary agendas more comprehensively, beyond the limitations set by formally determined debate themes (e.g., Debus & Tosun, 2021;Schmid, 2021;Schmidt et al., 2019), as members of parliament can also address energy issues in debates that are not primarily focused on energy. As mentioned above, members may speak on an energy issue in an unrelated debate in order to signal their political positions (Ivanusch, 2023) by creating a link between the ongoing, hitherto unrelated, debate and energy. This can happen, for example, when an opposition party criticises the government's fiscal policy in general and uses the opportunity to open a debate on an energy issue, such as taxing fossil fuels. This may be categorised under the heading 'fiscal policy' in the parliamentary register or by a researcher using a categorisation scheme. Thus, in this case, a study based on formal debate categories would have omitted the energy issue that existed within a fiscal policy debate. We see this as an important step towards uncovering true agendas in parliamentary debates and thus the temporal importance of specific policy issues.

With reference to previous studies on Finnish energy politics, we found that the debates had a consensual focus regarding energy solutions (see also Ruostetsaari, 2017;Vikström et al., 2023). It should be noted that consensus here does not refer to unanimity among the parties (which is beyond the scope of our methodology), but rather should be understood as common points of interest in the political debate. For example, when the overall national energy solution dominated the agenda in Finnish parliamentary energy debates in 2010, this should be interpreted as a sign of a shared political interest in the issue, not as an indication of an agreement on the concrete measures to be taken regarding the future energy mix. Topic modelling is a blunt instrument for the latter task. However, it is quite efficient in showing what the debate is about but requires additional analysis to examine interests. The same can be said for studying party differences. While the Greens emphasised a broad green transition and the Centre Party members appealed to agrarian voters by promoting the role of wood as an energy source, we can only judge their policy positions by knowing contextually where the parties' strategic interests lie. These differences were small and did not call into question the overall thematic consensus between the parties. Therefore, our methodology is limited in capturing the dimension of political dissent, that is, whether the speaker was for or against the topic of the debate. Thematic consensus is therefore not a measure of true party cohesion or cross-party alliances (cf., Proksch & Slapin, 2012). Relatedly, it leaves open the question of the possible manipulation of parliamentary speaking rules by party leaders to silence dissenting voices (Giannetti & Pedrazzani, 2016). Thus, our study was less concerned with agenda control than with the agenda itself and its changing structure. For agenda control, a different research strategy should be devised, such as an interview-based study that takes into account the larger institutional field of energy policy and various actor positions (Kainiemi et al., 2020). Considering this, we would encourage researchers to combine various methodologies in the future-for example, topic modelling of large sets of textual data together with a more nuanced contextual analysis. However, as mentioned above, the advantage of topic modelling compared to interviews or manual coding and/or the use of predefined categories (cf., Gava et al., 2017;Little, 2023) is its comprehensiveness. No relevant part of the parliamentary speech escapes the researcher's attention while the method provides a richer account of policy debates, revealing agendas that might otherwise remain uncovered. It is also possible to locate agenda shifts more precisely. A case in point is climate change mitigation, which became a dominant agenda in Finnish parliamentary debates in 2015-2016.

In our case, an interesting contextual factor was that energy agendas seemed to follow changes in government coalitions despite the sector being characterised by strong industry interests (e.g., Jensen-Eriksen, 2022; Lehtonen, 2020), rigid infrastructures, and strong path dependencies. Our findings imply that elections can indeed influence the direction of debates on energy politics even if the field is characterised by long-term continuity. Our data revealed stable topics with election-related change patterns that point to shared interests in energy issues by the entire parliament at a given point in time. Despite the political opportunities for interest signalling created by the diverse energy field and the relatively high number of parties in the parliament, the topics behaved in a regular manner. This finding needs to be considered with care and further research with a longer period is needed.

When analysed as topics, parliamentary debates reveal a rather consensual albeit also pluralistic and diverse policy field in terms of energy issue competition. Some party differences can be observed, but parliamentarians seem to take more liberties in their speeches compared to when they vote as part of the government coalition or the opposition, thus revealing that energy politics in the parliament is much more than a competition of predetermined interests that follow party positions.
